segments.s493-g65: کنون is an adverb ('now') setting the time. شاد و ایمن are predicate adjectives ('happy and safe') that describe the implied subject (you) and are joined by the conjunction و. به ایوان is a prepositional phrase of destination; here به means 'toward/into'. خرام is a verbal or verbal-like imperative ('stride/prance') placed after the destination in poetic word order, giving the instruction 'go/enter with a proud stride.'
segments.s493-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing the cause ('because of the world's care'). تیمار گیتی ('the world's care') is an adverbial genitive phrase: تیمار = 'care/worry' and گیتی = 'world'. مبر is the negative imperative ('do not take' / 'let not be taken'), addressed to the same implied subject; هیچ نام = 'any name' where هیچ = 'no/any' negates نام = 'name/reputation', so the clause means 'let no reputation be taken/lost because of worldly cares.'
segments.s493-g9: به مردی is a prepositional/temporal phrase meaning 'in manhood' (به + noun), placed before the subject for emphasis. تو is the subject pronoun 'you'; گستاخ is a predicative adjective 'presumptuous/insolent'; گشتی is the verbal past/perfect 'you became/turned' (here fused with the adjective). چنین is an adverb of degree 'so/thus' modifying گستاخ گشتی.
segments.s493-g10: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'that…'. مهتر is a predicative noun 'chief/greater', followed by شدی, the past of شدن 'you became'. بر + زمان و زمین means 'over/on (the) time and (the) earth' — a prepositional phrase showing the domain in which the subject is said to have become greater.
segments.s493-g11: همه نیکویها is a nominal phrase 'all excellences / all good things' (subject or wished-for good). ز is the archaic/poetic preposition marking source or recipient (here functioning like 'to/for'), and یزدان‌شناس is 'one who knows God' (a pious person) used as the beneficiary/recipient in a wish-form expression.
segments.s493-g12: This line continues the wish: و (and) زو is a contracted/poetic 'از او' (from/for him) and دار here refers to 'abode/possession' (poetic use meaning 'his possession or dwelling'); تا introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that / until', followed by زنده باشی (subjunctive 'you may be alive' / 'you remain alive'), and سپاس ('praise' or 'be praised') closes the wish. The construction is elliptical — verbs and auxiliaries are compressed as is common in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s493-g13: تو گویی is 'you say/you utter' (subject + verb phrase). به دانش here means 'by/with knowledge' (به used adverbially: 'according to your learning'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that…'; گیتی مراست is 'the world is mine' (مراست = مرا است, 'belongs to me' or 'is mine'), a fused copular construction common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s493-g14: نبینم is first-person singular negative present/past meaning 'I do not see / I cannot see'. همی is a frequent particle in classical Persian used as an emphatic/continuous marker ('indeed / still'). گفت و گوی is a fossilized pair meaning 'speech / saying' (گفت و گو 'conversation' or 'spoken words') and گوی تو راست = 'your saying/speech is true' with راست functioning as the predicate adjective 'true.' The connective و links the clauses.
segments.s493-g15: کجا is the interrogative 'where / where to' beginning a rhetorical question; آورد is the past/preterite 'did bring' or 'brings', here 'what use/what value does (it) bring'. دانش تو = 'your knowledge', and بها is 'price / value' — the object of آورد: 'Where does your knowledge bring value?' (i.e. what good is your knowledge?).
segments.s493-g16: چو introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'when/if'; آیی is the present/subjunctive 'you come / you enter'; چنین 'thus/so' qualifies the manner; در دم اژدها literally 'into the dragon's breath' or 'at the dragon's moment' (در + دم, where دم can mean 'breath' or 'instant'), a poetic image meaning 'in the deadly moment/breath of the dragon.' The whole clause gives the circumstance in which the prior question applies.
segments.s493-g57: 'چنان دان' is an imperative-like expression: 'دان' here functions as 'know/consider' and 'چنان' (so/thus) modifies it — 'consider thus'. 'که' introduces the subordinate clause that follows; 'طینوش فرزند من' is a noun phrase (name + apposition 'my son').
segments.s493-g58: 'کم' here is an adverb meaning 'little/scarcely' modifying the verb 'اندیشد' (3rd person singular present: 'he thinks' or 'gives thought'). 'از' governs the nouns 'دانش و پند' and means 'of/about' (marking the domain of his thinking). 'من' is the genitive pronoun 'my' (literally 'of me').
segments.s493-g59: 'یکی' is the indefinite 'one'/'a certain'. 'بادسارست' is an opaque/epithetic word (an epithet attached to the following noun), here left untranslated as a descriptive epithet. 'داماد' is the head noun 'son‑in‑law/groom'. 'فور' is an adverb 'immediately/at once' placed after the noun group for emphasis.
segments.s493-g60: 'نباید که' is an impersonal prohibition 'it should not be that / one should not', introducing the clause with 'داند' ('he know' / 'he presume to know'). 'ز نزدیک و دور' is a prepositional phrase 'about/from near and far' (i.e., matters close and distant) giving the scope of knowledge referred to.
segments.s493-g61: 'که' again introduces a clause. 'تو با سکندر' is 'you with Alexander' (you together with Alexander). 'ز یک پوستی' literally 'from one skin' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'of one skin' or 'of the same flesh' (i.e., closely allied or of the same character).
segments.s493-g62: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'ایدونک' is an archaic/poetic particle (here functioning like 'perhaps' or an emphatic conditional marker). 'با او به دل دوستی' means 'you have friendship with him in your heart' — 'به دل' is an adverbial phrase 'in the heart', and 'دوستی' is the noun 'friendship/affection'.
segments.s493-g63: 'که' again introduces the explanatory clause. 'او' is 'he'. 'از پی' is a classical locution meaning 'in pursuit of' or 'following'. 'فور' here is 'a sudden outburst' (fury/surge) and 'کین' is 'vengeance/hatred'; together 'از پی فور کین' = 'pursuing a sudden surge of vengeance'. 'آورد' is past tense 'he brought' / 'he caused'.
segments.s493-g64: This clause repeats 'آورد' (brought/caused) to specify the result: 'به جنگ' = 'into war/into battle' (bringing something into conflict), 'آسمان بر زمین' = 'heaven against earth' (word order: genitive/agentive phrase placed before the verb), so the whole means 'he brought a war of the heavens against the earth.'
segments.s493-g25: تو is the emphatic 2nd-person pronoun placed before the imperative. ایمن بباش is a present imperative construction (بباش = be) meaning 'be safe.' و links the two imperatives; به شادی is a prepositional phrase ('in/with joy') and برو is the second imperative 'go.' Word order is pronoun + predicate, then coordinated verb phrase with preposition + noun.
segments.s493-g26: چو رفتی is a temporal subordinate clause ('when you have gone') with چو + past tense رفتی. یکی کار is an indefinite object phrase ('a certain work') placed before the verb, and برساز نو is an imperative / hortative instruction: برساز 'make/bring about' with نو qualifying it ('new'). The adjective نو follows the verb phrase, as is common in such poetic constructions.
segments.s493-g27: کزین پس is an idiomatic temporal phrase 'from this on/after this.' نیابی is the 2nd-person negative present/future of یابیدن ('you will not find/attain'), and به پیغمبری is the goal/state expressed with به ('to/unto prophethood'). The clause therefore says you will no longer attain the state of prophethood.
segments.s493-g28: ترا is the object formed by the enclitic 2nd-person suffix ت plus the object marker را (here given as two items). خاک is the nominative subject 'the earth/soil' and داند is the present/future of دانستن used here in the sense 'deem/consider/know.' که introduces the complement clause supplying the content of that knowing, with اسکندری as the predicate (the copula is omitted as usual).
segments.s493-g29: ندانم is the first-person negative of دانستن ('I do not know'); کسی را is the (indefinite) object with the object marker را; ز گردنکشان is a genitive/partitive expression 'from among the neck‑cutters' (گردنکشان = those who cut necks / rebels). The phrase as a whole sets up an indefinite subject modified by the following relative clause.
segments.s493-g30: که introduces a relative clause modifying کسی. از چهر او is a prepositional phrase 'from his face'; من is the explicit first‑person subject placed before the verb for emphasis; ندارم is the negative present of داشتن ('I do not have'); نشان is the object 'a sign/mark.' The clause thus states 'for whom I do not have a mark (from his face),' so the whole sentence means there is no such person among the neck‑cutters.
segments.s493-g31: نگاریده is a past participle/perfect form meaning 'painted' (here used as a verb). هم means 'also.' زین here functions as a demonstrative/identifier ('of this' / 'this') in context (note: the lemma list gives other senses); نشان is the direct object 'mark/token.' بر حریر is a prepositional phrase 'on silk' (بر = on/upon + حریر = silk).
segments.s493-g32: نهاده is a past participle 'placed/put' (the action is stated without repeating an explicit subject). به نزد is a compound prepositional expression 'near/at the side of' and یکی یادگیر is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain learner/pupil' (یکی = a certain; یادگیر = learner). Word order places the placed-object/locative phrase after the participle.
segments.s493-g1: This is a compact past-tense clause with verb first (poetic word order): بخندید is a past/pluperfective form 'laughed' used here in the sense 'laughed at'. قیدافه is the subject/name that follows the verb. از + noun phrase (از کار اوی) marks the cause/object of the laughter ('at/because of his deed'); اوی is a possessive/pronoun 'his'.
segments.s493-g2: This continues the list of objects of the laughter with ازان مردی 'at that man' (ازان = 'from/that; concerning that') plus و to link a second object. تند گفتار اوی is a genitival noun phrase 'his rapid/harsh speech': تند modifies گفتار, and اوی again marks possession ('his').
segments.s493-g3: بدو گفت is a short reporting clause: بدو 'to him' (dative) + گفت 'said'. The following phrase کای خسرو شیرفش is direct address: کای is the vocative 'O', followed by the name/epithet خسرو شیرفش. Note that گفت is used as the reporting verb; its ordinary sense 'to say' is intended (see senses).
segments.s493-g4: This is an imperative/admonition. به مردی مگردان is a negative imperative phrase: مگردان (magradān) = 'do not turn/convert/submit' with به مردی 'to a man' (i.e. 'do not abase/submit yourself before a mere man'). سر خویش کش is another clause with سر 'head' + خوی(ش) ('one's own' formed by خوی + suffix ش) + کش (imperative of کشیدن 'pull/hold'); idiomatically it means 'hold/keep your head' (keep your dignity). Word order is typical poetic juxtaposition of two imperatives.
segments.s493-g5: A negative clause: نه introduces negation. از فر تو here means 'by/through your far (royal glory)'; از marks the causal/source relation. کشته شد is the passive 'were killed' (شد as auxiliary 'be' used with the past participle)، and فور هند ('the sudden surge/force of Hind' or 'the swift host of India') names the agent/affected group. Word order is compact poetic: source/cause before passive.
segments.s493-g6: Parallel negative clause introduced by نه: دارای داراب 'the possessions/domain of Darab' (دارای = 'having/possessing' used as substantive here) و links the second item گردان سند 'the troop/hosts of Sind' (گردان = troop, سند = Sind). The clause continues the negation from the previous segment (i.e. 'nor was ...').
segments.s493-g7: که introduces a subordinate clause (here temporal/causal: 'when/for'), برگشت روز بزرگان دهر is an idiomatic phrase: برگشت روز 'the turning of the day/fortune' + بزرگان دهر 'the great ones of the age' (بزرگان = 'great ones', دهر = 'age/time'). The whole means 'when the fortunes of the world's great turned'.
segments.s493-g8: This gives the resulting comparison: ز اختر ترا 'from/of your star' (ز is the poetic 'from/of') with the 2nd-person suffix ت indicating 'your'. را is the topical/definite marker attached after ترا, and بیشتر بود بهر states the comparison 'was greater/more' (بود = 'was' in the past). بهر functions as 'for/with respect to' concluding the clause. Overall: 'your star was greater.'
segments.s493-g49: به introduces the object of the oath ('by' or 'to'); دادار دارنده is a two-word epithet in apposition (both nouns refer to God: 'the Creator, the Possessor/Sustainer'). سوگند is the noun 'oath' and خورد is the past tense verb used idiomatically with سوگند to mean 'took/swore an oath' (literally 'ate/took the oath'). Word order is preposition + object (God) + object noun (oath) + verb.
segments.s493-g50: بدین here functions as a prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (ب + دین used adverbially: 'by this [oath/means]'). مسیحا is a reference to 'the Messiah' (used as the substantive governed by بدین); و is the conjunction 'and'. گرد is an adverb/particle meaning 'around/about' or may mark motion/turning; نبرد is the noun 'battle' (here the complement of the clause). Together the phrase says roughly 'by this (oath) he did not flinch from battle' with a compact, poetic word order.
segments.s493-g51: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that' or 'which'. با is the preposition 'with' and governs the following coordinated list: بوم و بارست و فرزند تو ('Būm and Bārest and your son'); بارست here is a proper name. The preposition با applies to the whole coordination; تو is the possessive 'your' attached after فرزند as usual in Persian.
segments.s493-g52: بزرگان is the head noun 'the great ones / nobles'. که again introduces the relative clause 'who/that'; باشند is the verb 'are' (third-person plural), here used in a general descriptive clause. پیوند تو is a nominal phrase 'your bond/ally(ship)' or 'those allied to you' (پیوند as 'link, kinship/ally'), with the possessor تو following the noun as is typical in Persian.
segments.s493-g53: نسازم is a first-person present/future form with negative prefix ن- + سازم ('I will not make' / 'I shall not build'); جز means 'except' and از marks the source/material 'from/out of'. خوبی و راستی are the coordinated nouns 'goodness and truth', the only materials the speaker allows for making/acting.
segments.s493-g54: نه is the negative particle 'not'; اندیشم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I think / I will think' (here negated: 'I will not think'); از introduces the domain 'of/regarding' and کژی و کاستی are the coordinated nouns 'crookedness (wrongness) and deficiency'. The clause is a parallel negative clause to the previous one.
segments.s493-g55: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. سوگند شد خورده is a compound expressing a completed action: سوگند 'oath' + شد (auxiliary 'became' / 'was') + خورده (past participle 'eaten/taken') — together 'when the oath was taken'. قیدافه is the name of the speaker and گفت is the past tense 'said'. The sequence follows classical Persian narrative order: temporal clause → participial phrase → naming the speaker → speech verb.
segments.s493-g56: که introduces reported speech 'that'; این پند is 'this counsel/advice' (این = 'this', پند = 'advice'); بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'to you' or 'upon you'. نشاید is an impersonal/modal expression 'it is not fitting / should not' and نهفت is a past-participle/adjectival verb 'hidden/kept secret' — literally 'it is not fitting that this counsel be hidden from you.'
segments.s493-g41: فرستمت is a past tense verb with the object clitic -ت ‘you’: “I sent/ I send you.” بر here means “to/toward” and governs the following noun phrase. نیکوی is an adjectival form “good/fine,” and باز is the adverb “again” placed before جای “place/seat.” Word order is poetic/inverted but reads: “I sent you to the good place again.”
segments.s493-g42: تو باید چِه-clause: باید is a modal ‘must/ought,’ followed by the subordinating که and the subjunctive/optative باشی ‘you be.’ The noun phrase خداوند رای is a genitive/compound: خداوند ‘sovereign/lord’ + رای ‘counsel/plan,’ i.e. ‘the sovereign’s counsellor’ or ‘one of the sovereign’s counsels.’
segments.s493-g43: به پیمان uses به adverbially (’by/under’) with پیمان ‘covenant/pledge’: ‘by the covenant.’ که introduces the content of the oath. هرگز is the negative adverb ‘never,’ and به before فرزند is the preposition ‘to/against’ (here: ‘toward my son’).
segments.s493-g44: This is a list introduced by the preposition به ‘to/for’: شهر من ‘my city,’ خویش (here the fused form خوی + ش) ‘my kin/relatives,’ and پیوند من ‘my bond/allyship.’ The final من repeats the possessive for پیوند; the construction is emphatic enumeration of protected persons/things.
segments.s493-g45: نباشی is a negative optative/imperative form ‘do not be’ (2nd person). It governs the following nominal phrase: بداندایش‌گر is a compound noun meaning ‘one who brings knowledge/advice’ with a negative prefix (here ‘ill‑advising’ or ‘false‑informant’), and بدسگال is an adjective ‘ill‑omened/ill‑fated’ modifying that noun.
segments.s493-g46: نخوانی is a negative imperative/subjunctive of خواندن here ‘to call/summon’ (’do not call/summon’). The object مرا is split in the manuscript into م + را (me + object marker). جز introduces the exception ‘except,’ and همال means ‘porter/bearer’ — i.e. ‘do not summon me to the realm except as a porter.’
segments.s493-g47: Simple narrative sequence: proper name سکندر (subject) + past verb شنید ‘heard’ + direct object این سخن ‘this speech/these words.’ شاد شد is a result clause ‘became glad’ (شاد ‘happy’ + auxiliary شد ‘became’). The two verbs are sequential past actions.
segments.s493-g48: ز تیمار وز کشتن آزاد شد uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) ‘from/of’: ‘free from care and from killing.’ Here وز is a poetic pairing (و + ز) meaning ‘and from’; تیمار ‘care/worry,’ کشتن ‘killing’ (verbal noun), and آزاد شد ‘was freed/became free.’ The sense is ‘he was released from anxiety and from the threat of death.’
segments.s493-g33: برو is the singular imperative 'go'. راند is a perfect/past stem used here with an imperative/ jussive force 'drive off/expel' (a poetic usage where past form carries a commandive sense). هم 'also' attaches to what follows (or to the action) and حکم اخترشناس is the object 'the astrologer's decree' (حکم = decree/judgment; اخترشناس = astrologer). The order is imperative verb(s) then the object phrase.
segments.s493-g34: کزو is a fused relative/adverbial 'from which' (که + زو), referring back to حکم; ایمنی 'safety' is the grammatical subject of باشد (here third‑person subjunctive/optative or present: 'be / will be'); اندر هراس is a prepositional phrase 'in fear' that modifies ایمنی. Together the clause says that from that decree safety will be in/subject to fear.
segments.s493-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بخشنده شد is the verbal phrase 'became generous' (بخشنده predicate + شد 'became'). خسرو is the proper name (Khosrow); رای‌زن follows as an appositive/epithet 'counsel‑giver' or title attached to the king. Poetic word order places the predicate before the subject ('چو بخشنده شد خسرو').
segments.s493-g36: زمانه is the topical subject 'the age / the world (of the time)'. بگوید is the third‑person verb (here perfective/present-subjunctive flavor: 'says / proclaims / will say'); به مرد و به زن is a dative phrase 'to men and to women' marking the audience. The clause reports that the age's counsellor announces this to everyone.
segments.s493-g37: تو is topical/emphatic 'you'. تا here functions as a temporal/durative 'until / for (the time being)' introducing how long the name will be used. ایدری بیطقون is a proper name or epithet being assigned, and خوانمت is a first‑person verb with the enclitic object -ت and final -م: 'I call/address you' (here future/iterative sense 'I will call you [thus]'). The object clitic -ت is merged into خوانمت rather than standing separately.
segments.s493-g38: برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این 'upon this / on this very [token]'. هم 'also' emphasizes sameness or addition. نشان دور is a noun + postpositive adjective 'a sign/mark far (away)'. بنشانمت is verb + object clitic + 1sg ending 'I will seat/set you' (بنشان-م-ت), here future or resolutive 'I will place you'. The whole segment states that, on this very token, the speaker will set the addressee far away.
segments.s493-g39: بدان is an imperative formed with the prefix ب- + دان (root 'know') meaning 'know this'. تا introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that / in order that' and governs the subjunctive/negative نداند 'may not know'. کسی 'anyone' is the indefinite subject, راز تو is the object 'your secret'. The clause reads 'Know it so that no one may know your secret.'
segments.s493-g40: همان 'that same (person)' resumes the referent introduced above. نشنود is the negative present/subjunctive form of شنود/شنو 'hear' (third person: 'may not hear / should not hear'). نام و آواز تو is the coordinated object 'your name and your voice'. The clause forbids that same person from hearing the name or voice.
segments.s493-g17: بدوزی is the 2nd-person singular present (classical/persuasive) form built with the verbal prefix ب‑ + dوزی (دوختن) meaning “you sew/stitch.” The adverbial phrase به‌روز جوانی follows the verb and means “in the day/prime of youth” (به‌روز = “of the day/prime”; جوانی = “youth”), and کفن is the direct object “shroud.”
segments.s493-g18: فرستاده‌ای is the 2nd-person perfect/resultative form (“you have sent/you sent”), here continuing the description of the addressee's actions. سازی is used in the sense of a musical strain/piece (an instrumental/melodic product) ‘a strain’ or ‘tune,’ و از خویشتن literally “from/of yourself” marks the source (از = “from/out of”; خویشتن = emphatic reflexive “yourself”).
segments.s493-g19: مرا نیست is an existential/experiential construction where مرا (to me / for me) with نیست expresses “I do not have” or “it is not (to me).” آیین خون ریختن is a noun phrase meaning “the custom/rite of shedding blood” (آیین = custom/rite; خون = blood/bloodshed; ریختن used as a deverbal noun meaning “to shed”).
segments.s493-g20: نه introduces a second negated item “nor.” بر here functions as a preposition “upon/against/before,” خیره (plausibly ‘the gaping/onlookers’ or ‘the stunned’) names the audience/scene, با مهتر آویختن is a verbal phrase “to cling to / fawn on a great man” (با = “with/at”; مهتر = a great/elder/distinguished man; آویختن = “to cling to/attach oneself”), so the clause says one does not (moralistically) fawn openly on the powerful.
segments.s493-g21: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning “when.” شاهی here refers to the royal person/kingship in this context (classical usage), به کاری = “in regard to a task/affair” (به marking the domain), توانا is a predicate adjective “capable/able,” and بود is the past copula “was.” The clause sets a conditional/temporal background: “When the king was able in affairs…”
segments.s493-g22: ببخشاید is a past/remote narrative sense of بخشیدن (here “he granted/pardoned”); از before داد و دانا بودن works as a prepositional marker meaning roughly “of/for” and introduces the qualities: داد = “justice” (i.e. being just) and دانا = “wise.” The final بود is the copula “was,” so the sentence says the king pardoned and was just and wise.
segments.s493-g23: چنان دان is an injunction “consider/know thus” (چنان = thus/so; دان = imperative/verb ‘consider/think’). که introduces a following clause describing the subject: ریزنده خون شاه is a noun phrase “the king who sheds blood” (ریزنده = ‘one who scatters/sheds’; خون = blood(bloodshed); شاه = king).
segments.s493-g24: جز introduces an exception (“except/only”), آتش = “fire,” and نبیند is a subjunctive/negative form of دیدن used here to mean “will see” (in a proverbial/future sense) — so جز آتش نبیند = “will see nothing but fire.” به فرجام گاه is a locative temporal phrase “at the time/place of ending/the end,” completing the thought “in the end he will see only fire.”

sentences.s493-s33: Now, joyful and secure, stride into the iwan; let no reputation be lost to the world's cares.
sentences.s493-s5: In manhood you became so presumptuous that you became greater than time and earth.
sentences.s493-s6: May all good qualities be to the God-knower and to him; may you be praised so long as you live.
sentences.s493-s7: You speak, by learning, that the world is mine; I do not see it — yet your saying is true.
sentences.s493-s8: Where has your knowledge brought value, when you come thus into a dragon's breath?
sentences.s493-s29: Consider this: Tinush, my son, gives little thought to my knowledge and counsel.
sentences.s493-s30: A certain bādsārest son‑in‑law (groom) should not, at once, presume to know what is near and far.
sentences.s493-s31: That you are, with Alexander, of one skin — if perhaps you cherish friendship with him in your heart.
sentences.s493-s32: For he, pursuing a sudden outburst of vengeance, brought (it) — he brought the heaven's war down upon the earth.
sentences.s493-s13: Be safe and go off in joy; when you have gone, set up some new work.
sentences.s493-s14: From this onward you will not attain prophethood; the earth will deem you (to be) Alexander.
sentences.s493-s15: I know no one among the neck-cutters for whom I do not have a mark from his face.
sentences.s493-s16: He (or one) also painted this mark on silk and placed it beside a certain learner.
sentences.s493-s1: Qeydafeh laughed at his action — at that man and at his sharp speech.
sentences.s493-s2: He said to him, 'O Khosrow Shirafsh, do not bow to a mere man; keep your head up.'
sentences.s493-s3: It was not by your farr that the Indians were slain in a rush, nor (was it) Darab’s domain and the hosts of Sind.
sentences.s493-s4: For when the fortunes of the age's great ones turned, your star was greater than theirs.
sentences.s493-s25: He swore by the Creator, the Sustainer; by this oath he did not shrink from battle.
sentences.s493-s26: that with Būm and Bārest and your son — the nobles who are allied to you.
sentences.s493-s27: I will make nothing except out of goodness and truth; I will not think in terms of crookedness and deficiency.
sentences.s493-s28: When the oath had been taken, Qeydafeh said that this counsel ought not to be concealed from you.
sentences.s493-s21: I send you back to that fine place; you must be the sovereign's counsellor.
sentences.s493-s22: On oath: that you shall never (do harm) to my son, to my city, to my kin, and to my bonds.
sentences.s493-s23: Do not be an ill-advising, ill‑omened informer; do not summon me to the country except as a porter.
sentences.s493-s24: Alexander heard these words, rejoiced, and was freed from care and from killing.
sentences.s493-s17: Go — drive away the astrologer's decree as well, for from it safety will be in fear.
sentences.s493-s18: When Khosrow became generous, the age's counsellor proclaimed it to men and to women.
sentences.s493-s19: I will call you Aidri-Biṭqūn for now; on this same token I will set you far away.
sentences.s493-s20: Know this so that no one may learn your secret; that same person should not hear your name or your voice.
sentences.s493-s9: You stitch a shroud in the prime of youth; you have sent forth a strain from yourself.
sentences.s493-s10: It is not my custom to shed blood, nor to fawn openly on a great man.
sentences.s493-s11: When a king was able in action, he pardoned; he was just and wise.
sentences.s493-s12: Consider this: a king who sheds blood will see nothing at the end but fire.
